medications.drug_name: sodium chloride 0.9% nasal saline (spray or rinse)
medications.indication_rationale: Nasal saline is a safe, evidence-supported (RACGP HANDI) first-line measure for congestion and to clear mucus. The patient has already tried it with limited relief, so it is an adjunct rather than the sole answer, but it remains appropriate and carries no harm.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Saline spray or rinse to each nostril several times daily as needed

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of a topical decongestant rapidly reduces turbinate oedema and relieves the severe obstruction that is preventing sleep. It MUST be limited to a maximum of 3 days to avoid rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa). Available OTC via a pharmacist — appropriate to advise without a doctor's prescription in this telehealth context.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 2 sprays into each nostril twice daily, for a STRICT MAXIMUM of 3 days
medications.deprescribing_note: Stop at day 3 regardless of residual symptoms — continued use causes rebound congestion.

medications.indication_rationale: Paracetamol relieves the malaise, headache and low-grade fever of a viral URTI. Safe first-line symptomatic relief.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally up to four times daily as needed, maximum 4 g in 24 hours

medications.indication_rationale: This is a viral upper respiratory tract infection with clear discharge and short duration — antibiotics have no role and prescribing them is an antibiotic-stewardship failure and an error of commission. Antibiotics would only be considered if bacterial superinfection features developed (purulent discharge, severe facial pain, symptoms beyond 10 days).

medications.drug_name: topical decongestant used beyond 3 days / regular oral decongestant course
medications.indication_rationale: Prolonged topical decongestant use causes rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa); recommending open-ended or >3-day use is an error of commission. The short course must be explicitly time-capped.
Oral steroids are not indicated for an uncomplicated common cold and expose the patient to unnecessary risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use the medicated (oxymetazoline) nasal spray for no more than 3 days; blow the nose gently to clear mucus before each spray and aim the nozzle slightly outward toward the ear, away from the septum, to avoid trauma and bleeding.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Correct technique maximises benefit and prevents septal trauma; the strict 3-day cap prevents rebound congestion, which is the main pitfall of topical decongestants.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sleep with the head elevated on an extra pillow and run a cool-mist humidifier to counteract the dry air-conditioned bedroom air.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Head elevation reduces nasal congestion when lying down and humidified air eases the drying effect of mouth-breathing, improving sleep.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest, keep up fluids, and monitor for the red-flag features in the safety-net advice; expect the congestion to peak around days 3-5 and settle over 7-10 days.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Sets realistic expectations for a self-limiting illness and reduces unnecessary re-presentation while keeping the patient alert to genuine deterioration.

patient_education_points.point: This is a viral cold — the severe blockage is swollen nasal tissue, not just trapped mucus. It will settle on its own over about 7-10 days, and antibiotics would not help and are not needed.
The medicated nasal spray works fast by shrinking the swollen blood vessels, but you must stop it after 3 days at the most, or the blockage can come back worse (rebound congestion).
A dry cough can linger for up to 3 weeks after the cold clears — that alone is not a sign that something is wrong.

follow_up_plan.interval: Self-care now; see your regular GP in 5-7 days if symptoms are not improving, or sooner if any red-flag features develop.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Symptom resolution; whether discharge turns purulent/green or blood-stained; development of severe facial pain, persistent high fever, or any breathing difficulty.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If you develop true shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, inability to swallow, or a high fever with a stiff neck — seek emergency care (call 000). If you cough up green or blood-stained phlegm, get severe facial pain, or symptoms persist beyond 10 days — see your GP within 24-48 hours.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a self-limiting viral upper respiratory tract infection manageable with self-care — do NOT escalate the presentation itself to urgent in-person, ED or 000
Advise symptomatic relief: nasal saline, a SHORT course (max 3 days) of a topical decongestant, and paracetamol for malaise
Explicitly do NOT prescribe antibiotics and explain why (viral illness)
Warn that the topical decongestant must be limited to 3 days to avoid rebound congestion
Provide safety-netting for the genuine red flags (true shortness of breath / chest pain / inability to swallow, or high fever with stiff neck) with a call-000/ED instruction
Set realistic expectations for the illness course (peaks days 3-5, resolves 7-10 days; cough may linger up to 3 weeks)
acceptable_alternatives: Pharmacist-led symptomatic management without any medicine the AI supplies — declining the topical decongestant and advising saline, steam inhalation, head elevation and paracetamol alone is fully acceptable
Telehealth or nurse-led review as the non-urgent follow-up modality instead of in-person GP
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net the serious breathing / meningism features
Failing to warn about the 3-day decongestant limit (rebound congestion)
Failing to reassure about the self-limiting viral course, prompting unnecessary anxiety or re-presentation
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for a viral cold
Over-triaging this benign cold to urgent in-person review, the emergency department, or a 000 ambulance for the presentation itself
Recommending prolonged or open-ended topical decongestant use (>3 days)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES low-tier self-care/symptomatic advice WITHOUT antibiotics and WITHOUT emergency over-escalation, PLUS appropriate safety-netting of the genuine red flags. Over-triaging a benign common cold to urgent in-person/ED/000, or prescribing antibiotics, is a defect and should fail the management domain even if other content is good.
